notes: One A4-sized (double-sided) hardcopy Cheat Sheet allowed.

questions.label: Question 1
questions.text: Demonstrate your understanding of class hierarchy. What will be the output of the following code snippet?
questions.explanation: Due to Python name mangling, self.__value in Parent becomes _Parent__value (=5 from super().__init__()), while self.__value in Child becomes _Child__value (=10). When c.display() is called, super().display() accesses _Parent__value=5, and Child's own part accesses _Child__value=10. Output: 'Parent value: 5  Child value: 10'.
questions.modelAnswer:
C — Parent value: 5  Child value: 10

Python name mangling means `self.__value` in each class refers to a different attribute:
- `Parent.__init__` sets `_Parent__value = 5`
- `Child.__init__` calls `super().__init__()` (sets `_Parent__value = 5`) then sets `_Child__value = 10`
- `super().display()` inside Child reads `_Parent__value = 5` → 'Parent value: 5'
- Child's `{self.__value}` reads `_Child__value = 10` → 'Child value: 10'
keyPoints: Name mangling: self.__value in Parent → _Parent__value, in Child → _Child__value
super().__init__() sets _Parent__value = 5
Child.__init__ then sets _Child__value = 10
super().display() reads _Parent__value = 5

questions.label: Question 2
questions.text: This question tests your understanding of attributes and methods. Which line/s will raise an error?
questions.explanation: Line 19 (rect.width = 10) attempts to set the @property 'width', but no setter is defined — this raises AttributeError: can't set attribute. Because line 19 raises an error, line 20 (print(rect.area())) is never reached, making both lines problematic.
questions.modelAnswer:
B — Line 19 and 20

The `width` property only has a getter (`@property`), no `@width.setter`. Attempting to assign `rect.width = 10` at line 19 raises `AttributeError: can't set attribute`. Since execution halts at line 19, line 20 is never reached.
keyPoints: @property without a setter creates a read-only attribute
rect.width = 10 raises AttributeError: can't set attribute
Line 20 cannot execute because line 19 already raised an error

questions.label: Question 3
questions.text: Demonstrate your understanding of object composition. Which line demonstrates composition?
questions.explanation: Composition (HAS-A relationship) is shown when one class contains an instance of another class as an attribute. 'self.__engine = engine' stores an Engine object inside Car — Car HAS-A Engine. This is composition: the Engine object is passed in and owned by Car.
questions.modelAnswer:
B — self.__engine = engine

Composition means one class HAS-A reference to an object of another class. `self.__engine = engine` stores an `Engine` instance inside `Car`, meaning Car is composed of an Engine. This is the HAS-A relationship that defines composition.
keyPoints: Composition = HAS-A relationship: one class owns an instance of another
self.__engine = engine stores an Engine object inside Car
Contrast with inheritance (IS-A): class Car(Engine) would be inheritance

questions.label: Question 4
questions.text: Apply OOP principle. Which part of the code enforces the requirement for subclasses to implement specific methods?
questions.explanation: The @abstractmethod decorator is what enforces that any subclass of AbstractVehicle MUST implement the decorated method. If a subclass does not implement it, Python raises TypeError when you try to instantiate it. Inheriting from ABC alone does not enforce method implementation.
questions.modelAnswer:
B — @abstractmethod

The `@abstractmethod` decorator marks a method as abstract, meaning any concrete subclass MUST provide its own implementation. If a subclass fails to implement all abstract methods, Python raises `TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class`. Simply inheriting from `ABC` makes the class abstract but does not enforce method contracts — `@abstractmethod` does.
keyPoints: @abstractmethod enforces that subclasses must implement the method
Failing to implement raises TypeError on instantiation
ABC (Abstract Base Class) marks the class as abstract
@abstractmethod on method + ABC on class = contract enforcement

questions.label: Question 5
questions.text: Apply OOP principle. What will be printed when 'child_method' is called?
choices: An error occurred in Child.  An error occurred in Parent.
An error occurred in Parent.  An error occurred in Child.
questions.explanation: child_method() calls self.parent_method() first, which raises ValueError('An error occurred in Parent.'). This exception propagates immediately — the subsequent raise in child_method never executes. The outer try-except catches it and prints 'An error occurred in Parent.'
questions.modelAnswer:
D — An error occurred in Parent.

`child_method()` calls `self.parent_method()` first. `parent_method()` raises `ValueError('An error occurred in Parent.')`. This exception propagates up immediately — execution never reaches `raise ValueError('An error occurred in Child.')`. The `except ValueError as e` block in the main code catches it and `print(e)` outputs 'An error occurred in Parent.'
keyPoints: parent_method() raises ValueError before child_method() can raise its own
The first raise stops execution — the second raise is never reached
Exception propagates up the call stack to the outer try-except
print(e) prints the string message of the caught exception

questions.label: Question 6
questions.text: Apply OOP principle. What will be the output when it is executed?
choices: TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class Cat with abstract method 'sound'
questions.explanation: Cat inherits from Animal(ABC) but does not implement the abstract method 'sound' — it just has 'pass'. Trying to instantiate Cat() raises TypeError because abstract methods must be implemented in concrete subclasses.
questions.modelAnswer:
C — TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class Cat with abstract method 'sound'

`Animal` is an abstract class with `@abstractmethod sound()`. `Cat` inherits from `Animal` but never implements `sound()` — it only has `pass`. When `Cat()` is called, Python raises `TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class Cat with abstract method 'sound'`.
keyPoints: Cat inherits Animal(ABC) but does not implement sound()
pass in Cat body means sound() is still abstract
Instantiating a class with unimplemented abstract methods raises TypeError
Dog works because it implements sound() with return 'Bark'

questions.label: Question 7
questions.text: Apply OOP principle. Analyze the code below and determine which modification would correctly define the 'Bird' class with the expected output: 'Flapping wings'.
questions.explanation: The abstract method is named 'fly'. bird.fly() is called, and print() wraps the call in main. If fly() itself calls print(), then print(bird.fly()) would print 'Flapping wings' then 'None'. The code calls print(bird.fly()), so fly() should return the string, not print it... BUT looking at the output expected is just 'Flapping wings' from print(bird.fly()). If fly() returns the string, print() outputs it correctly. Answer C (print inside fly) would output 'Flapping wings\nNone'. Answer A (return string) with print(bird.fly()) outputs 'Flapping wings'. So answer is A.
questions.modelAnswer:
A — def fly(self): return "Flapping wings"

The main code calls `print(bird.fly())`. If `fly()` returns the string 'Flapping wings', then `print()` outputs it cleanly. Option C (`print` inside `fly`) would cause double output: 'Flapping wings' from fly's print, then 'None' from print(bird.fly()). The abstract method is named `fly` so options B and D (using `flap`) would not satisfy the abstract contract.
keyPoints: Abstract method is named fly — implementation must be def fly(self)
print(bird.fly()) calls print on the return value
fly() should return the string, not print it (to avoid 'None' output)
Implementing the wrong method name (flap) does not satisfy the abstract contract

questions.label: Question 8
questions.text:
Examine the following code intended to demonstrate method overloading that can accept 2 or 3 arguments. Identify the issue in the code that will prevent it from functioning as intended and propose a way to fix it.

```python
class Operation:
    def multiply(self, x, y, z):
        return x * y * z

    def multiply(self, x, y):
        return x * y

op = Operation()
result = op.multiply(3, 4)   # Expected output: 12
result = op.multiply(3, 4, 2) # Expected output: 24
```

Write your fixed code for the Operation class.
keyPoints: Python does not support method overloading — same name = second overwrites first
Fix: use a default parameter (z=1) so the method works with 2 or 3 args
Alternative: use *args to accept variable number of arguments
Must explain the bug AND provide the corrected code for full marks

questions.label: Question 9
questions.text:
You are developing a system for a logistics company to manage different types of shipments. Each type of shipment calculates shipping cost differently based on the shipment's weight and includes a base cost associated with handling specific shipment types.

Requirements:
1. Create an abstract class 'Shipment' with an abstract method 'calculate_cost'.
2. Use class variables to define base costs and additional fees for each type.
3. Implement subclasses 'RefrigeratedShipment' and 'PriorityShipment'.

Expected Output:
```
Shipment ID 101 to New York: Cost = $25.00
Shipment ID 102 to San Francisco: Cost = $27.50
```

subQuestions.label: Question 9a
subQuestions.text:
Construct the class with the following specifications:
- Define an abstract class named 'Shipment'
- An '__init__' method that accepts 'id', 'weight', and 'destination'
- An abstract method 'calculate_cost'
- A class variable 'base_cost' set to 1.5
- Getter for 'weight' using @property decorator
keyPoints: from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
class Shipment(ABC): — inherits ABC
base_cost = 1.5 as class variable (not instance variable)
__init__ accepts id, weight, destination and stores as private attributes
@property getter for weight returning self.__weight
@abstractmethod on calculate_cost

subQuestions.label: Question 9b
subQuestions.text:
Construct the subclasses 'PriorityShipment' and 'RefrigeratedShipment'. Each class should:
- Implement the 'calculate_cost' method to include the additional fees
- 'PriorityShipment': class variable priority_fee = 10
- 'RefrigeratedShipment': class variable refrigeration_fee = 20
- Expected outputs: PriorityShipment(101, 10, 'New York') → Cost = $25.00; RefrigeratedShipment(102, 5, 'San Francisco') → Cost = $27.50
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Working backwards from the expected output:
- PriorityShipment(101, 10, 'New York'): Cost = $25.00
  → 10 * base_cost + priority_fee = 10 * 1.5 + 10 = 25.00 ✓
- RefrigeratedShipment(102, 5, 'San Francisco'): Cost = $27.50
  → 5 * base_cost + refrigeration_fee = 5 * 1.5 + 20 = 27.50 ✓

```python
class PriorityShipment(Shipment):
    priority_fee = 10

    def calculate_cost(self):
        return self.weight * Shipment.base_cost + PriorityShipment.priority_fee


class RefrigeratedShipment(Shipment):
    refrigeration_fee = 20

    def calculate_cost(self):
        return self.weight * Shipment.base_cost + RefrigeratedShipment.refrigeration_fee
```
keyPoints: PriorityShipment.priority_fee = 10 as class variable
RefrigeratedShipment.refrigeration_fee = 20 as class variable
calculate_cost = weight * base_cost + fee (derive from expected output)
Use self.weight (the property getter) not self.__weight
Both classes must implement calculate_cost() to be concrete

subQuestions.label: Question 9c
subQuestions.text:
Implement a '__str__' method in the Shipment class that fulfils the expected output. Use 'id' and 'destination' to identify each shipment.

Expected output format:
Shipment ID 101 to New York: Cost = $25.00
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
```python
def __str__(self):
    return f"Shipment ID {self.__id} to {self.__destination}: Cost = ${self.calculate_cost():.2f}"
```

Note: Since __id and __destination are name-mangled private attributes, you may need to access them as _Shipment__id and _Shipment__destination, or store them with single underscore or provide property getters.
keyPoints: Format: 'Shipment ID {id} to {destination}: Cost = ${cost:.2f}'
:.2f formats the float to 2 decimal places
Calls self.calculate_cost() — polymorphic, works for both subclasses
Must be defined in Shipment base class, not subclasses

questions.label: Question 10
questions.text:
Building upon the previous shipment system, extend it to include a tracking system using composition.

The Tracking class (provided) is:
```python
class Tracking:
    def __init__(self):
        self.__history = []

    def update_status(self, status):
        formatted_datetime = datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
        self.__history.append((formatted_datetime, status))

    def get_history(self):
        if not self.__history:
            raise Exception('No tracking history available.')
        return self.__history
```

Modify the 'Shipment' class to incorporate 'Tracking' using composition. Implement 'start_tracking', 'update_status', and 'get_status_history' methods. Include exception handling where necessary.

Expected output:
```
Status History: [('2024-12-29', 'Initialized'), ('2024-12-29', 'Awaiting Pickup')]
```
questions.modelAnswer:
Add the following to the Shipment class:

```python
from datetime import datetime

class Shipment(ABC):
    base_cost = 1.5

    def __init__(self, id, weight, destination):
        self.__id = id
        self.__weight = weight
        self.__destination = destination
        self.__tracker = None  # Tracking object, None until started

    def start_tracking(self):
        self.__tracker = Tracking()
        self.__tracker.update_status('Initialized')

    def update_status(self, status):
        if self.__tracker is None:
            raise Exception('Tracking has not been started. Call start_tracking() first.')
        self.__tracker.update_status(status)

    def get_status_history(self):
        if self.__tracker is None:
            raise Exception('Tracking has not been started.')
        return self.__tracker.get_history()

    # ... existing weight property, calculate_cost, __str__ ...
```

Usage:
```python
refrigerated_shipment = RefrigeratedShipment(103, 8, 'Chicago')
refrigerated_shipment.start_tracking()            # Creates Tracking, adds 'Initialized'
refrigerated_shipment.update_status('Awaiting Pickup')
print(refrigerated_shipment.get_status_history()) # [('2024-12-29', 'Initialized'), ...]
```
keyPoints: Composition: self.__tracker = Tracking() stored as instance attribute in Shipment
start_tracking() creates a new Tracking() and calls update_status('Initialized')
update_status() delegates to self.__tracker.update_status()
get_status_history() delegates to self.__tracker.get_history()
Exception handling: raise Exception if tracker is None (not started)
from datetime import datetime needed for the Tracking class

questions.label: Question 11
questions.text: Develop a simple tkinter GUI for the logistics system allowing users to select a shipment, view its details, and update its status.

subQuestions.label: Question 11a
subQuestions.text:
The sample code shows a Tkinter window with a Listbox to select a shipment and Label widgets to display current status and details. Lines 16 and 17 are missing — complete the code to create the GUI window.

The completed code will show a window titled 'Shipment Tracker' with a Listbox showing shipments and a 'Current Status: N/A' label at the bottom.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Lines 16 and 17 create the main tkinter window:

```python
win = tk.Tk()                        # Line 16: create root window
win.title("Shipment Tracker")        # Line 17: set window title
```

Full context of the missing lines in the ShipmentTrackerGUI.__init__:
```python
def __init__(self, shipment_list):
    self.__shipment_list = shipment_list
    win = tk.Tk()                    # Line 16
    win.title("Shipment Tracker")    # Line 17
    self.__listbox = tk.Listbox(win)
    self.__listbox.pack()
    for shipment in self.__shipment_list:
        self.__listbox.insert(tk.END, f"{shipment.destination} (ID: {shipment.id})")
    self.__status_label = tk.Label(win, text="Current Status: N/A")
    self.__status_label.pack()
    self.__listbox.bind('<<ListboxSelect>>', self.on_select)
    win.mainloop()
```
keyPoints: Line 16: win = tk.Tk() — creates the root window
Line 17: win.title('Shipment Tracker') — sets the window title
tk.Tk() must be called before any other tkinter widgets
win.mainloop() at the end starts the event loop

subQuestions.label: Question 11b
subQuestions.text: Ensure the GUI can dynamically update the current status. The goal is to display the current status (shipment.get_status_history()) when the cursor selects the listbox. ONE line is missing in the on_select method.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
The missing line updates the status label with the selected shipment's tracking history:

```python
self.__status_label.config(text=f"Current Status: {shipment.get_status_history()[-1]}")
```

Full on_select method:
```python
def on_select(self, event):
    selected_index = self.__listbox.curselection()
    if selected_index:
        shipment = self.__shipment_list[selected_index[0]]
        # missing line:
        self.__status_label.config(text=f"Current Status: {shipment.get_status_history()[-1]}")
```

The expected output shows 'Current Status: ('2025-01-14', 'Initialized')' — the last tuple from get_status_history().
keyPoints: self.__status_label.config(text=...) updates the label text dynamically
shipment.get_status_history()[-1] gets the most recent status entry
[-1] index retrieves the last element from the history list
The label displays the tuple e.g. ('2025-01-14', 'Initialized')
